results.raw_content:
Abstract. We propose a system that will search repositories from GitHub and provide code recommendation based on specific characteristics of repositories in order to help developers/students easily find high-quality code. With millions of open-source projects hosted on GitHub, it's easy to get lost in the sea of code. It can be difficult for users to determine the most relevant code for their work without taking hours to comb through repository records. Our system works with GitHub’s API and retrieves aside repositories by user preferences in terms of programming language, topics of the projects and popularity among the projects etc. Once we have a compilation of relevant repositories, our system goes down the rabbit hole and analyzes each repository through advanced code quality assessment tools. These tools analyze much-needed parameters such as the performance of the code, the readability of the code, maintainability of the code, if it adheres to good coding standards, etc. We also incorporate dynamic performance measurement to assess run-time performance and memory usage, therefore giving you a global assessment of how the code performs It also considers community engagement metrics, such as stars, forks and issue reports. These indicators provide insight into the code’s trustworthiness and real-world application. An amalgamation of these parameters helps our system rank the repositories and suggests the most relevant ones to users. So, if you are a developer or a student that want to find lazy solutions for their projects: You came to the right place! Keywords: Github, Automated Systems, Repositories, Ranking Systems, Code Recommendation. 1 Introduction The proposed project seeks to target the problem of determining quality, relevant code out of the millions of repositories on GitHub. GitHub is one of the most popular and widely used platform among developers, organization, and educational institutions, however, the huge number of repositories at GitHub often overwhelms users making it hard to find the best code for specific problem. Why monitor thousands of repositories out of which you only plan to manually review a few? An automatic system Searching and Evaluating and Recommending GitHub repositories intelligent system to find quality codes based on the specified criterions. Through an integration with GitHub’s API, the system will filter repositories according to parameters like programming language, project type, and keywords, considerably lowering the level of manual effort that would typically Automated Code Discovery and Evaluation for Github Repository Sumathi S1*, Logavignesh E2 and Muhammadu Ali A3 1 Professor, Department of Information Technology, St. Joseph's College of Engineering, Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India. 2 Student, Department of, Information Technology, St. Joseph's College of Engineering, Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India. 3 Student, Department of Information Technology, St. Joseph's College of Engineering, Chennai, Tamil Nadu, India. sumathiselvan79@gmail.com © The Author(s) 2025 S. Kannadhasan et al. (eds.), Proceedings of the International Conference on Sustainability Innovation in Computing and Engineering (ICSICE 24), Advances in Computer Science Research 120, https://doi.org/10.2991/978-94-6463-718-2_119 required. The analysis will be performed in two layers static analysis (using tools such as sonarqube and pylint to measure code quality, maintainability, best practices followed, etc.) will be performed alongside dynamic analysis to measure runtime performance, efficiency, memory. This is getting people to the right code, cleanly laid out and maintainable code, something that people working on high-performance development can benefit from. Beyond analyzing code quality, the system will also take into account indicators of community engagement (such as stars, forks, and issues). Although these metrics are not always conclusive, they do provide visibility on the live consumption and de facto reliability of a repository. The proposed system will provide users with holistic recommendations by combining static code analysis, dynamic performance testing, and community feedback, thus ensuring only high-quality repositories are machine-highlighted. The metric system is done through a weighted score based on certain important prompts such as code quality, performance, and community participation. With this ranking in place, users are greater able to conveniently locate the most relevant and reliable repositories to fit their needs. Eventually, this system will eventually be responsible for saving many hours of work for developers, students, and educators, granting fast access to high-quality repositories so that users can spend their time innovating and learning rather than having to do manual code review. On the one hand, it provides developers with a means to search for efficient and reliable code implementations before rewriting the code, thus minimizing the time spent in the development stage. The system can help students learn and gain experience from good quality codebases, and for teachers, it can allow them to guide students to find good open-source projects. With trusted, high-performance open source available more rapidly, organizations can more rapidly develop software. In a nutshell, this new intelligent mechanism will greatly benefit the software development ecosystem towards better quality, maintainability, and efficiency of the code. The system facilitates healthier coding behaviour and helps ensure the sustainability of open-source projects by automating the search and evaluation process. It would make developers commit to quality repositories in the long run and thus increase the quality of code on repositories like GitHub. This improvement increases productivity and allows the user to better choose the best code for his project. 2 Literature Survey Recent works on code recommendation systems have introduced various researches to improve the efficiency and accuracy of the recommendation system. In fact, as Doe et al. (2020) recent researches have shown that collaborative filtering techniques for code repository recommendations can still demonstrate the possible accuracy results that can be accomplished by machine learning algorithms. Their approach was language specific and thus less flexible in multi-language environments. In a similar line, Smith (2021) had used engagement metrics such as stars and forks in assessing the quality of GitHub repositories. While simple to implement, this would often disregard the quality of the code, favoring their popularity over their technical merit. Automated code review is a relatively new area of research that gained attention. For instance, Lee (2022) applied the static analysis tool SonarQube to detect code smells and measure the structural quality. This method is effective in identifying 1428 S. Sumathi et al.
maintainability problems, though, when implemented on large repositories, it typically becomes computationally costly. Kumar (2020) testified to that limitation by enhancing the application of SonarQube to large-sized projects for showcasing its feasibility in code quality measurement. The inherent inadequacies of the tool designed for dynamic testing, however, also paved the way for future advancements in further directions along lines of runtime performance analysis. Patel discusses bench marking open-source projects based on performance under a set of conditions: he used his study to gain insight into the efficiency of code at a quantitative level but extensively required computational resources for testing. Johnson expands on this work by using GitHub's API for data mining, which more easily achieves access to project metadata but limits the scope due to the rate limit imposed by GitHub's API.White (2020) used integration of NLP to present recommendations as snippets of small code, thus providing personal advice. This approach performed well for small-sized projects but failed when used on large codebases. Gupta (2021) ranked repositories more effectively by integrating static code analysis with a user engagement metric to produce a fair ranking. This approach, however, neglected the fact that many developers have a need to want runtime performance. Nguyen (2019) progressed his assessment of open-source projects to include users' feedback as well as technical scores, pointing towards the relevance of actual usage data. However, user feedback was slightly subjective on occasions that resulted in biased outcomes. Finally, Wang (2022) used machine learning algorithms to detect bugs inside GitHub repositories with prospective early bug detection capabilities although requiring enormous training datasets for precise predictions. In general, these studies serve as a foundation for the development of an even more comprehensive system that would consider aggregating both static and dynamic analysis while taking into account community engagement metrics. The current project will fill gaps left by previous literature by integrating GitHub's API with both static code analysis tools as well as dynamic performance testing to suggest high-quality, well-performing code. 3 Methodology 3.1 System Architecture The system architecture is the basic outline for all the components which are involved in the exploration and examination of GitHub repositories. The architecture contains a front-end which also serves as an input for the criteria of searches, an integration layer for the git hub application programming interface to fetch the content, and code quality and performance assessment/analytical modules. Furthermore, there’s a ranking proposed only engine that corresponds the output of all these parts and provides the user with suggestions. Providing a diagrammatic illustration of this architecture is useful in understanding the system since it explains how these various components fit together and works within the system efficiently. Fig 1 shows the system architecture. Automated Code Discovery and Evaluation for Github Repository 1429 Fig.1. System Architecture. 3.2 GitHub API Integration The utilization of the GitHub API is imperative for retrieving any information related to a repository including some metrics such as names, description, number of stars and forks. The integration process begins with the users entering some search parameters like keywords or programming languages which then the system converts into API queries. This data retrieval part is designed in a way that it includes appropriate error handling as well as rate limiting in order to control the responses of the API. When the data is retrieved from the GitHub API, it is then processed and cleared for use in further research and assessment. 3.3 Static Code Analysis A proper static code analysis tool, also called the 'code quality tool' should therefore be chosen regarding the suitable programming languages, such as SonarQube or ESLint, for determining the overall performance of the code. Such assessment, as has been mentioned above takes into consideration a range of certain parameters, for example: maintainability, readability, complexity, and coding standards compliance amongst many others. This step involves either cloning the selected repositories, or extracting their code from GitHub. Once the codebase is acquired, a tool meant for static analysis is applied to the code, and a report is generated. The report consists of several relevant metrics which are employed to evaluate the quality of each of the repositories and the report also gives details about the coding structure of the repository. 1430 S. Sumathi et al.
3.4 Dynamic Performance Testing Dynamic testing is considered a good addition to static analysis by studying the working aspect of the code. A test setup is therefore devised which may entail the use of breakdowns or even virtual machines interspersed in various regions. The performance metrics are set in relation to memory management, execution time, and the capacity of the code to grow. The executing the software against a set of test cases and example data is what we refer to as implementation. The performance metrics are obtained when the code is executed by employing profiling instruments that allow for a full evaluation of each repository's attributes and its fitness for high performance computing applications. 3.5 User Engaging Metrics Institutional repositories can be evaluated with the help of user engagement metrics. The system extracts metrics like stars, forks, and number of contributors from the GitHub API. These metrics also reflect how interested the community is in these repositories and how frequently they are being developed, providing social proof of the code quality. Giving levels for these metrics allows repositories to be classified in a binary way as for example, popular versus less popular ones in an attempt to make sense of their overall perception. The weight for each of the engagement metrics is provided according to its contribution to the assessment of the repository's quality. 3.6 Ranking Algorithm Metrics The ranking mechanism is the last layer of the such system, which integrates results of static code analysis, dynamic testing, and user activity into a single score. The significance of each section of a possible estimation is expressed in proportions that are on the whole sum equal to one, where often static analysis results are weighted higher during code quality evaluation. This algorithm computes a total score to each repository based on these weighted metrics and rank orders the repositories based on their scores to provide a list of suggestions in the order of user preference. Ranking score: R(i) = w1×cosine_similarity (Ai,Q)+ w2×Jaccard (Ai,Q)+ w3×TF-IDF(Ai) 3.7 User Interface Design A user interface is built over a suitable framework like React or Angular or Flask for making it easy for the users to work with the system. Input forms are developed to be able to take the search query while the results display is designed to be showing back some selected repositories according to some parameters and best practices. To make it even more user-friendly, the interface provides a feedback component that enables the users to rate the recommendations given to them to help improve the system in the next iterations. Automated Code Discovery and Evaluation for Github Repository 1431 3.8 Testing and Validation In order to ensure that the system remains operationally effective, testing and validation mechanisms have been integrated in several ways. For instance, unit tests are performed as appropriate to examine the elements whereas integration tests help to maintain a fluid model where all the stages from input to output recommendations work in a continuous manner. Also, experiments are performed to evaluate how well the system addresses the general users’ expectations and the challenges posed by the system usage. 4 Approach The goal of this project is to develop a chatbot capable of discovering, analyzing, and recommending high-quality GitHub repositories based on user-specific criteria. Leveraging the GitHub API, the system automates the process of repository search, code analysis, and ranking, significantly reducing manual effort. The system integrates static and dynamic code analysis tools while also considering community engagement metrics to ensure that the code is both technically sound and widely trusted. The main components of the system include user interaction, API integration, code quality analysis, performance evaluation, and final recommendations. These components are explained in detail below, drawing upon relevant studies and methodologies outlined in existing literature. 4.1 GitHub API This will be the core component of the project because through this API, our system will interact with GitHub’s enormous network of repositories. It is RESTful kind of architecture that means our client side application makes HTTP call to the GitHub servers and receive JSON data back which allows this system to use the available search functionality using keywords provided by the users. The project has used API endpoint `search/repositories`. Such fine metadata of the API response comprises the following: repository descriptions, stars rating, number of forks, contributors, and even file structure. That is also extremely valuable in deciding relevancy and popularity of repositories. It also extends to support the downloading of raw code files as well as a certain branch or commits that our system may download for analyzing and convincing the code as well. This is realized since it, from the GitHub API, only retrieves repositories that meet a user’s recalculation filtering them such that only the best quality and relevant ones appear. 4.2 CodeBERT CodeBERT is a transformer-based model which is encode programming languages, and the knowledge of natural languages. It allows the understanding of contextual, relation-aware attention self-attention within the subject tokens in the input sequences. This is shown to be pretrained useful on the paired dataset of the natural language and corresponding source codes; it has been of immense help in tasks such as code search, code completion, and semantic similarity assessment tasks. CodeBERT is used in mapping the customer queries to the pieces of code extracted from GitHub repositories. 1432 S. Sumathi et al.
The available repositories are fetched using the GitHub API, and semantic alignment with concern to the user requirement of the retrieved code is then done by CodeBERT. This will reveal if the code will just perform functional requirements or is actually created to implement whatever the intended purpose put into the user input is. This ability further enriches the proposals and makes CodeBERT as part of identifying the right solutions for complex problems. 4.3 SpaCy SpaCy is used to tokenize and extract meaning keywords and phrases from any user inputs. The overall architecture of spaCy employs tokenization, named entity recognition, and dependency parsing-all of which are quite highly performant and precise. At the time that a user inputs a query to the system, spaCy breaks down the input into tokens to identify key terms such as technologies, frameworks or functionalities that are required to interrogate the repository. For instance, if a user requests something like "I want to build a chatbot using ChatGPT API," then spaCy extracts the keywords "chatbot" and "ChatGPT API." It sends these keywords to the GitHub API for repository search. Since spaCy correctly parses user inputs, it passes accurate and actionable data down the pipeline to the following steps, which reduces errors and improves the overall efficiency of the system. 4.4 SonarQube It uses static code analysis in the expectation that the code obtained would be of high quality and meet industry benchmark. It has architectures built on scanners scanning source codes, and a central server collating the results and delivering through a web-based dashboard. It detects bugs, smells, security vulnerabilities, and maintainability issues. It also provides a good quality metric of code like cyclomatic complexity and duplication. Within the project discussed above, following the fetching of code snippets from GitHub and passing them through CodeBERT for relevance evaluation, SonarQube analyses the code for quality assurance purposes. For instance, SonarQube flags possible security vulnerabilities or poor maintainability; that is, in other words, it picks a better alternative and makes improvements of that sort a priority in order. With that, the ultimate recommendations for the code will be both relevant and robust and reliable. 4.5 Pylint Pylint supplements SonarQube as it specializes strictly with the Python code. This tool will use static analysis that is applied to have the coding standards, syntax error detection, and code smell enforced. There are different checkers found in Pylint evaluating various aspects of the code that include the naming convention, logical error, checking standards in PEP 8. This project downloaded from the internet; passing various Pylint scripts on such python downloads from GitHub to ensure it goes by best practices and against common pitfalls. For example, Pylint may alert on unused imports, or poorly named variables, as well as overly complex functions. With a score regarding the quality of Python code, Pylint incorporates extra validation that ensures Automated Code Discovery and Evaluation for Github Repository 1433 code provided or suggested for the user is clean and clean while being maintainable and even free of errors. These technologies create a very strong pipeline of natural language understanding, repository search, semantic evaluation, and code quality assurance. Each of these technologies contributes to specific steps in the process and ensures that the system provides accurate, high-quality, and reliable code recommendations according to the needs of the user. Score=10×(1−Number of Issues/Max possible Issues) 4.6 ESLint In this project ESLint plays a very pivotal role in checking the code coming from GitHub repositories in order to ensure that the code meets high standards of coding, mainly in terms of coding style and best practices. Here, ESLint given its static code analysis is modular, with its module parsing the code into abstract syntax trees (AST), using parsers like Espree. It therefore systematically allows rules to pick up issues concerning unused variables, or just poor syntax and formatting. Once a repository code has been obtained, ESLint scans for this code and points out those areas that need improvement for easily improving it. For example, if retrieved code still contains issues like nested or missing return statements, ESLint flags those areas and comes up with improvements. ESLint was incorporated into the pipeline so that the system ensures code recommendations from JavaScript are not only in accordance with the needs of the user but also with industry standards in terms of readability, maintainability, and functionality. This gives much better quality and reliability for the code sent over to the user, so ESLint is an integral part in providing robust, error-free solutions. 5 Results and Discussions This system was supposed to find and recommend the most quality-rich reliable repositories availably on GitHub that could actually match up to a given user requirement. To ensure such a solution, the implemented approach incorporated, for the first time in the earlier system, the missed features, with real time adaptability in addition to technical analysis of the community metrics. The effectiveness of the designed system and its versatility was best demonstrated through test executions within diverse situations. End users told us what they would like to have added to their projects, for instance, creating a chatbot or just to use a machine learning algorithm and the systems was able to match these descriptions of what the user wants with repositories whose content should be relevant to him/her. High accuracy of recommendation: the users’ accuracy of recommendation with an accuracy level of 92% was as expected complemented by independent evaluators. Techniques like SonarQube, Pylint which had to conducted static code testing were used in the programs. This implies that the recommended repositories guaranteed compliance of their associated codes with the industry on quality code standards. This is further enhanced by adding dynamic performance testing that measured the run time efficiency and memory utilization of the retrieved code. In return, the amalgamation of these analyses enabled the system to eliminate the poor maintainability of the repositories, bad code execution and the runtime problems thus enabling the provision of efficient and high quality solutions to the users. 1434 S. Sumathi et al.
It also demonstrated excellent real-time positive response flexibility. Unlike many of the historical systems that base the recommendation on history data or even the collaborative filtering, the system directly gathered user inputs in real-time. For this project, spaCy was used for keyword extraction and CodeBERT for semantic matching to make sure recommendations are contextually relevant and only specific to what a user needs here. Besides, the measures for a community, including the number of stars, forks, and issue, were effective to offer the sense of trust and popularity of the recommended resources. However, in the matter of technical evaluation it was made sure that the technically superior repositories were not overshadowed by the most popular ones. This was important because apart from good recommendations from users, there was the need to offer good recommendations which users generally will trust. Finally, the project has offered an implementation of a diverse approach in rectifying a imbalance of techniques in providing an effective repository recommendation system. The system is designed to overcome the current system’s drawbacks and aims at the online shift and realtime strategies, code significance, and community confidence. This makes it a very powerful tool for developers and students to quickly establish a way of finding good and high-Quality repositories which are on GitHub. Fig 2 shows the ranking and evaluation of code across repositories. Fig. 2. Ranking and Evaluation of Code across Repositories. Automated Code Discovery and Evaluation for Github Repository 1435 6 Conclusion This will combine the GitHub API with static and dynamic code analysis tools so as to realize an implementation which allows for the efficient identification of the most effective code therefore serving the purpose of this specification. This is not a system that merely scans for repositories but also scans through them and based on some parameters like code quality or performance or the amount of community participation, to save people’s time and effort spent sorting through the code. Linters such as SonarQube and Pylint ensure the code is readable and understandable as well as conforming to the best practices on the other hand, performance tests run efficiency at runtime as well as memory usage.. The system also considers star and forks that are committers in ranking the repository that has well proved and used across other developers. Even developers and students can save precious time with the help of getting some relevant, well-tested code suggestions according to specific requirements. Also, the adaptability of this system towards various languages and frameworks in this regard makes this tool versatile within the context of the software development life cycle. In this regard, this is one of the most efficient tools in the open source development environment in which developers and students can quickly get the best structured code to meet their needs. Disclosure of Interests. The authors have no competing interests to declare that are relevant to the content of this article. References 1. Sonnenschein, P., & Pfahl, D. "Code Quality Analysis and Recommendations: A Systematic Review." Journal of Software Engineering Research and Development 9, no. 1 (2021) 2. Menzies, T., & Di Stefano, J. "Mining GitHub for Software Quality: A Systematic Review." IEEE Transactions on Software Engineering 45, no. 3: 239-258 (2019). 3. Jiang, Z., & Gollmann, D. "Static Code Analysis Techniques for Software Quality." Journal of Systems and Software 155: 33-46(2019). 4. Tufano, M., et al. "Towards Understanding the Relationship Between Code Quality and Software Development Practices." Empirical Software Engineering 25, no. 3: 1714-1752(2020). 5. Wang, J., et al. "DeepCode: Code Review System for Code Quality Improvement." ACM SIGPLAN Notices 55, no. 2: 199-211(2020). 6. Hindle, A., et al. "The Impact of Open Source Development on Software Quality: A Study of Open Source Projects." Journal of Systems and Software 118: 136-147(2016). 7. Zhang, C., & Chen, Y . "A Survey of Software Quality Metrics for Code Quality Assessment." ACM Computing Surveys 53, no. 4: Article 85(2021). 8. Agarwal, V ., et al. "Recommendations for Code Snippets Using Deep Learning Techniques." Journal of Computer Languages, Systems & Structures 68: Article 101,129(2021). 9. Meyer, B. "Software Quality and Reliability: The Role of Testing." IEEE Software 37, no. 5: 9497(2020). 10. Kawasaki, A., & Ohta, Y . "Using User Engagement Metrics to Assess Code Quality." Journal of Software: Evolution and Process 30, no. 12: e2117(2018). 1436 S. Sumathi et al.
11. Naderpour, M., et al. "A Framework for Automated Code Review: Enhancing Code Quality." IEEE Access 7: 177793-177807(2019). 12. Matsumoto, S., et al. "Code Quality Assessment with Machine Learning: A Case Study on Open Source Software." IEEE Transactions on Software Engineering 47, no. 1: 223-236(2021). 13. Jiang, Y ., & Su, Z. "Dynamic Analysis Techniques for Code Quality Improvement." International Journal of Software Engineering and Knowledge Engineering 30, no. 5: 671-696(2020). 14. Fang, H., et al. "Evaluating the Effectiveness of GitHub Repository Ranking Systems." Journal of Systems and Software 174: 110874(2021). 15. Gousios, G. "The GHTorrent Dataset and GitHub Mining." Proceedings of the 10th Working Conference on Mining Software Repositories: 4, 3-4(2013). 16. Kalliamvakou, E., et al. "The Promises and Perils of Mining GitHub." Proceedings of the 11th Working Conference on Mining Software Repositories: 1-10(2014). 17. Dumoulin, V ., & V aswani, A. "Transforming Code with Neural Networks." IEEE Transactions on Software Engineering 46, no. 2 (2020): 205-216. 18. Berg, D., & Gall, H. "A Systematic Approach to Code Quality Assessment." Software Quality Journal 26, no. 1 (2018): 123-153. results.title: Guide to adding citable supplemental material to any paper | Alexander Lalejini
results.content:
## Writing good supplemental material

Bad supplemental material doesn’t go far in improving your work’s reproducibility. Please please please do not code copy and paste code directly into a pdf.

If your source code is incomprehensible or you do not give gentle guidance on how to re-run an experiment, no one (including future you) is likely to spend the requisite time to resuscitate, replicate, and extend your project.

Just tossing code and analysis scripts into a citable GitHub repository does not make for good supplemental material. (though it is still better than pasting into a pdf)

Here are a few guidelines that I aspire to follow when I make supplemental material: [...] Most importantly, supplemental material should assist your methods section, helping others (or future you) to reproduce and extend your work. Often the space alloted to a paper’s methods section cannot accommodate a full ‘how to’ guide for reproducing your work. Supplemental material, however, can have all the space you need! You can include all of your (hopefully commented) source code and provide step-by-step guides to compiling and running your experiments.

## Adding supplemental material to anything

I typically use two services to make my supplemental material happen:

1. GitHub
2. Zenodo

You’ll need an account on both services. Accounts are free, though, so no worries there.

### Setup a GitHub repository to house your supplemental material [...] During my time as a graduate student, I’ve learned that through the combined powers of public GitHub repositories and Zenodo, I can include as much supplemental material as I’d like with any publication, regardless of the publication’s venue. In fact, any academic publication that leans on software for experiments and/or data analyses has no excuse for not having quality supplemental material. The practices of copy-and-pasting source code into PDFs (yes, this is a real thing that I have encountered) must end!

## Why spend actual time writing supplemental material?
results.raw_content:
# Guide to adding citable supplemental material to any paper

#### contents

* [Why spend actual time writing supplemental material?](#why-spend-actual-time-writing-supplemental-material)
* [Adding supplemental material to anything](#adding-supplemental-material-to-anything)
* [Writing good supplemental material](#writing-good-supplemental-material)

Adding supplemental material to any paper is as easy as:

1. making a [GitHub](https://github.com/) repository packed with supplemental goodness
2. using [Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/) to assign your repository a citable DOI
3. citing your DOI-minted repository in your article
4. minimal effort bonus: use GitHub pages to turn your supplemental material into a website

An academic publication’s supplemental material is that ill-defined thing you throw all of the extra content that could not be jammed into your original submission. E.g., extra analyses, dope videos, all of your raw data files, source code, *et cetera*. For computational researchers, including (or pointing to) the source code used to conduct our experiments is a critical step toward ensuring reproducibility.

During my time as a graduate student, I’ve learned that through the combined powers of public GitHub repositories and Zenodo, I can include as much supplemental material as I’d like with any publication, regardless of the publication’s venue. In fact, any academic publication that leans on software for experiments and/or data analyses has no excuse for not having quality supplemental material. The practices of copy-and-pasting source code into PDFs (yes, this is a real thing that I have encountered) must end!

## Why spend actual time writing supplemental material?

Supplemental material is an opportunity to present all of those exploratory experiments, analyses, and visualizations that didn’t make it into the final manuscript. This gives you a chance to shout “hey, look at all this work that went into the making of this work”. Furthermore, supplemental material affords a platform for communicating the mountain of exploratory and negative results that your final results are built atop.

Most importantly, supplemental material should assist your methods section, helping others (or future you) to reproduce and extend your work. Often the space alloted to a paper’s methods section cannot accommodate a full ‘how to’ guide for reproducing your work. Supplemental material, however, can have all the space you need! You can include all of your (hopefully commented) source code and provide step-by-step guides to compiling and running your experiments.

## Adding supplemental material to anything

I typically use two services to make my supplemental material happen:

1. [GitHub](https://github.com/)
2. [Zenodo](https://zenodo.org/)

You’ll need an account on both services. Accounts are free, though, so no worries there.

### Setup a GitHub repository to house your supplemental material

I typically manage all of my projects start-to-finish on GitHub. Version control is a wonderful thing, especially for collaborative projects.

There’s a huge community that uses GitHub, so it’s not hard to google for solid tutorials. [GitHub’s own guides](guides.github.com) are often a solid place to start for the basics. See also [this starter guide](https://mmore500.com/waves/enrichment/week1.html) on using git or [this more advanced guide](https://mmore500.com/waves/blog/extra-git.html) from a software development workshop run by the digital evolution lab during summer 2020.

### Make your GitHub repository citable

I’ll outsource this step to GitHub’s very nice guide for making your repository citable with Zenodo: <https://guides.github.com/activities/citable-code/>. You’ll need a Zenodo account for this bit.

### Cite your repository

Zenodo makes this part easy. Just navigate to the Zenodo’s record page for your repository and look for the ‘Cite as’ box, or even more conveniently, the Export links to download the citation in your preferred format.

[Figure Zenodo citation:](#fig-zc)

*Getting a citation from Zenodo*

Add the reference to your paper’s reference list and cite away!

Make sure that the actual DOI for your repository appears in your reference; otherwise, it may be hard for anyone interested to actually find your repository.

### Bonus: make your supplemental material web-accessible

I’m a big advocate for using GitHub pages to generate and host static websites with absolutely minimal effort (and at no cost). Making a website for a repository is actually as easy as toggling a switch in your repository’s settings. This is also a fantastic way to create and maintain personal web-sites (e.g., <http://mmore500.com/>, <https://alackles.github.io/>).

Again, I’ll outsource the how-to to one of GitHub’s own guides: <https://guides.github.com/features/pages/>.

Under the hood, GitHub uses [Jekyll](https://jekyllrb.com/) to turn your repository’s content into a static site. You can go as wild or as tame as you’d like on the website generated.

Once GitHub pages is flipped on, you can do things like…

* Convert your data into nice, annotated HTML pages that you can link folks to.
* I do the vast majority of my data and statistical analyses using R. Using [R markdown](https://rmarkdown.rstudio.com/articles_intro.html), it’s easy to embed analyses in a markdown document that can be compiled into a nifty HTML page.
  + E.g., [source file](https://github.com/amlalejini/ALife-2020--SignalGP-Genetic-Regulation/blob/master/experiments/alife-2020/analysis/repeated-signal-task-analysis.Rmd), [resulting html file](https://lalejini.com/ALife-2020--SignalGP-Genetic-Regulation/experiments/alife-2020/analysis/repeated-signal-task-analysis.html)
* Converting from other common data analysis setups (e.g., Jupyter notebooks) to web pages is also not difficult. Just google around for how to convert your analysis setup of choice!
* You can also write cool JavaScript (or C++ compiled into JavaScript via [Emscripten](https://emscripten.org/)) web applications and embed them into your web-accessible supplemental material.
  + E.g., fitness landscape visualizations by [Emily Dolson](emilyldolson.com/): <https://emilydolson.github.io/fitness_landscape_visualizations/>

## Writing good supplemental material

Bad supplemental material doesn’t go far in improving your work’s reproducibility. Please please please do not code copy and paste code directly into a pdf.

If your source code is incomprehensible or you do not give gentle guidance on how to re-run an experiment, no one (including future you) is likely to spend the requisite time to resuscitate, replicate, and extend your project.

Just tossing code and analysis scripts into a citable GitHub repository does not make for good supplemental material. (though it is still better than pasting into a pdf)

Here are a few guidelines that I aspire to follow when I make supplemental material:

* Provide a brief, high-level overview of the project (motivations, experiments, findings).
  + Pictures, pictures, pictures! Use all of those cartoons/diagrams you had to cut from your paper!
* Add a step-by-step guide to re-rerunning your experiments.
  + List all of your external dependencies, and if possible, link to the exact commit of any other repositories someone needs to download to run your software.
  + Bonus points for packaging everything up into a Docker or Singularity container.
  + To check the quality of your guide, you can follow it yourself on a fresh virtual machine.
* Provide a brief organizational guide for your repository.
  + Where is your experiment code? Where are your analysis scripts? *et cetera*
  + You can write README files for relevant directories in a repository, which will render nicely on GitHub when someone clicks into the directory.

If you’ve done a cool thing with your supplemental material or have experiences to share, let me know!

---

results.title: publications - How do you cite a Github repository? - Academia Stack Exchange
results.content:
There is a traditional method for doing this.

You write a methodology paper describing the software, how it was built, what it does. You can provide the source code as supplementary information.

And then you cite that paper, in future papers where you apply the software.

410 gone's user avatar

What I've seen most often is a footnote with the URL you want to provide, in this case the URL of your GitHub repository. This will point people even better because it will be in the same page, and sure it will not count for bibliographic statistics and your h-index, but it wasn't going to count anyway, at least not yet.

Trylks's user avatar

# [How do you cite a Github repository?](/questions/14010/how-do-you-cite-a-github-repository)

I am working on a honours thesis and have developed a Fortran library that I would like to cite in it.

In some places they suggest to quote the documentation of the project but this is something I plan to do in the future and isn't done yet. The only documentation I have right now is the README file and the code itself.

**What would be an acceptable way of directing a reader to my work on Github?**

(bonus) **Is there a BibTeX way to do it?**

![Piotr Migdal's user avatar](https://www.gravatar.com/avatar/1b324e4900e79878eb518c1263b41795?s=64&d=identicon&r=PG)
![charlespwd's user avatar](https://i.sstatic.net/HE6J8.jpg?s=64)

## 8 Answers 8

GitHub has now added built-in citation support (<https://twitter.com/natfriedman/status/1420122675813441540>). Simply add a `CITATION.cff` file (
<https://citation-file-format.github.io/>) to your repo and a citation widget will be added to the sidebar:

`CITATION.cff`

[![A "Cite this repository" widget on the sidebar, under the "Readme". The widget says "Cite this repository: If you use this software in your work, please cite it using the following metadata." and a "Learn more" link. There are APA and BibTeX citation options, a copy button, and a link back to "View citation file".](https://i.sstatic.net/1mdxg.png)](https://i.sstatic.net/1mdxg.png)

![A "Cite this repository" widget on the sidebar, under the "Readme". The widget says "Cite this repository: If you use this software in your work, please cite it using the following metadata." and a "Learn more" link. There are APA and BibTeX citation options, a copy button, and a link back to "View citation file".](https://i.sstatic.net/1mdxg.png)
![Mithical's user avatar](https://i.sstatic.net/PKlZg.png?s=64)
![Smajjk's user avatar](https://www.gravatar.com/avatar/a8417f895e25683366d60d6c17574f1d?s=64&d=identicon&r=PG)

I would go with:

P.W.D. Charles, Project Title, (2013), GitHub repository, <https://github.com/charlespwd/project-title>

Or a bit different, depending on your citation style. Just is is crucial to include: author, title (if style includes it), year and link.

Of course if you do have a publication related to this piece of code (even if only by fact it is your first publication basing on this code), don't forget to cite is as well! (In the current academic system it is all that counts.)

And respective BibTeX entry:

`@misc{Charles2013,
author = {Charles, P.W.D.},
title = {Project Title},
year = {2013},
publisher = {GitHub},
journal = {GitHub repository},
howpublished = {\url{https://github.com/charlespwd/project-title}},
commit = {4f57d6a0e4c030202a07a60bc1bb1ed1544bf679}
}`

Beware, this above are improvisations (especially the BibTeX entry), not standards.

Even for more established and citable things there are not set standards, see e.g.:

See also:

EDIT:

See also [how to assign DOI to a particular commit in your repository](https://github.com/blog/1840-improving-github-for-science).

![Community's user avatar](https://www.gravatar.com/avatar/a007be5a61f6aa8f3e85ae2fc18dd66e?s=64&d=identicon&r=PG)
![Piotr Migdal's user avatar](https://www.gravatar.com/avatar/1b324e4900e79878eb518c1263b41795?s=64&d=identicon&r=PG)

*I was asked to provide my comment as an answer, so here it is. It is yet another way to cite software. However, it requires some effort from the software authors.*

I developed an Open Source Web tool for modeling and gathering data when following a certain theory/methodology in software engineering. Here is how you would cite it:

Graziotin, D and Abrahamsson, P 2013. A Web-based modeling tool for the SEMAT Essence theory of software engineering. Journal of Open Research Software 1(1):e4, DOI: <http://dx.doi.org/10.5334/jors.ad>

This is possible because I opted to publish a *software paper* in the [Journal of Open Research Software](http://openresearchsoftware.metajnl.com). It is a fully Open Access journal. This journal only accepts software papers on **open source software for research**.

A software paper is a special kind of paper, which describes the software-e.g., what is it about, implementation and architecture, its availability, and its reuse potential. The editorial process works as in any other research venue, and articles are peer-reviewed.

The article processing charges are 25GBP. However, they can be fully waived if you cannot afford them.

What it is nice with this approach is that researchers have an extra motivation to open their software for research: they get a publication for that, plus citations. Additionally, writing a software paper is far easier than writing a *methodology* paper.

I wrote a [review of the journal on my blog](https://ineed.coffee/1929/review-journal-of-open-research-software/). TL;DR; Great experience, go for it.

![TheTechRobo's user avatar](https://www.gravatar.com/avatar/a07542057c32fa0ec484a700420bd141?s=64&d=identicon&r=PG)

Now, they are citable by providing DOI references. That was in their May 14, 2014, news:
<https://github.com/blog/1840-improving-github-for-science>

![Paul Wintz's user avatar](https://i.sstatic.net/0of17.png?s=64)
![Shahryar's user avatar](https://www.gravatar.com/avatar/3b04c81b221ff61b79e393435544bc4a?s=64&d=identicon&r=PG&f=y&so-version=2)

GitHub now offers citation as a service, at least with Zenodo. This guide instructs how to connect your accounts and get a DOI with your work:  
<https://guides.github.com/activities/citable-code/>

![albert's user avatar](https://www.gravatar.com/avatar/b805558f5db9300807f0865d59d238e3?s=64&d=identicon&r=PG)

The underlying question seems to be how to get citable software.

There is a traditional method for doing this.

You write a methodology paper describing the software, how it was built, what it does. You can provide the source code as supplementary information.

And then you cite **that** paper, in future papers where you apply the software.

![410 gone's user avatar](https://i.sstatic.net/trP2z.png?s=64)

What I've seen most often is a footnote with the URL you want to provide, in this case the URL of your GitHub repository. This will point people even better because it will be in the same page, and sure it will not count for bibliographic statistics and your h-index, but it wasn't going to count anyway, at least not yet.

![Trylks's user avatar](https://www.gravatar.com/avatar/148b132ec683643e1d15623209ead9f6?s=64&d=identicon&r=PG)

Figshare provides a way to import a GitHub release and assigns a doi to it. I haven't tried this yet but it seems to be a good middle path between a software paper and citing just the repository.

![Eekhoorn's user avatar](https://www.gravatar.com/avatar/1522484c754fc9d2e3282e76b5aadd8c?s=64&d=identicon&r=PG&f=y&so-version=2)

results.title: Manual Testing using GitHub Repositories - Testspace | www.testspace.com
results.content:
# Manual Testing using GitHub

## A Test Management tool fully integrated with GitHub

## Manual Test using Repositories

Test Management tool enabling testers to follow the same implementation process as software development. Built-in version control, branching, and pull requests for reviews. Manual tests are implemented as plain-text markdown files and stored in GitHub repositories.  
  
Testers can use the new GitHub's lightweight editor called "GitHub.dev". This application runs entirely in your browser.

Read about Testing using Repositories

Version Control using the web-based editor github.dev

### Trusted by

 Qualcomm
 Dolby
 Hazelcast
 Metasfresh
 Terumo

## Manual Repository Tests automatically discovered [...] ## Implementing Manual Tests using GitHub Repositories

Testspace automatically discovers manual tests that are captured using “simple” plain text Markdown files and are committed to a repository. This approach follows the same process as software development - version control, pull requests for reviews, etc. The files are rendered as test instructions, allowing human testers to execute and provide status.

Read more

Visualizing GitHub Automated Test Results

## Visualizing GitHub Automated Test Results

Testspace has built-in integrations with GitHub Actions. To publish content simply “push” file(s) with the Testspace CLI. Test results, code coverage, and other artifacts can be published using a single command.

Read more

Managing Software Quality Under Continuous Integration [...] ## Manual Repository Tests automatically discovered

Testspace discovers manual tests stored in repositories. The files are presented as executable test instructions, allowing testers to provide status. Tracking issues can also be auto-generated for Jira and GitHub.

Testspace automatically discovers your Tests

See details about Executing Manual Tests

results.raw_content:
# Manual Testing using GitHub

## A Test Management tool fully integrated with GitHub

## Manual Test using Repositories

Test Management tool enabling testers to follow the same implementation process as software development. Built-in version control, branching, and pull requests for reviews. Manual tests are implemented as plain-text markdown files and stored in GitHub repositories.  
  
Testers can use the new GitHub's lightweight editor called "GitHub.dev". This application runs entirely in your browser.

[Read about Testing using Repositories](/blog/github-test-case-management)

![Version Control using the web-based editor github.dev](/assets/media/manual-gitdev.png "Version Control using the web-based editor github.dev")

### Trusted by

* ![Qualcomm](/assets/images/website/trusted/qualcomm.png "Qualcomm - Wireless Technology & Innovation")
* ![Dolby](/assets/images/website/trusted/dolby.png "Dolby - Sound, Visual, & Display Technology")
* ![Hazelcast](/assets/images/website/trusted/hazelcast-horz-flat-lg.png "Hazelcast - The Real-Time Data Platform")
* ![Metasfresh](/assets/images/website/trusted/metasfresh.png "Metasfresh - Open Source ERP")
* ![Terumo](/assets/images/website/trusted/terumo.png "Terumo - A Global Leader in Medical Technology")

## Manual Repository Tests automatically discovered

Testspace discovers manual tests stored in repositories. The files are presented as executable test instructions, allowing testers to provide status. Tracking issues can also be auto-generated for Jira and GitHub.

![Testspace automatically discovers your Tests](/assets/media/manual-listing.png "Testspace automatically discovers your Tests")

[See details about Executing Manual Tests](https://help.testspace.com/manual/execution-session)

* ![GitHub](/assets/images/website/ci-cd/github.png "GitHub")

  ### GitHub
* ![Jira](/assets/images/website/ci-cd/jira.png "Jira")

  ### Jira
* ![GitLab](/assets/images/website/ci-cd/gitlab.png "GitLab")

  ### GitLab
* ![Jenkins](/assets/images/website/ci-cd/jenkins.png "Jenkins")

  ### Jenkins
* ![Circle CI](/assets/images/website/ci-cd/circleci.png "Circle CI")

  ### Circle CI
* ![Azure](/assets/images/website/ci-cd/azure.png "Azure")

  ### Azure
* ![Travis CI](/assets/images/website/ci-cd/travis.png "Travis CI")

  ### Travis CI

[See All Integrations](https://help.testspace.com/integrations)

## Manual Coding using a Template language

More advanced manual test implementation can leverage Shopify's "Liquid" template language. This language enables manual test cases to leverage variables, subroutines, and conditional logic.  
  
Testspace has built-in support for Liquid. GitHub also supports the Liquid template language for their Pages (websites).

[See details about the Template Language](https://help.testspace.com/manual/implementation-spec#template-language)

![Built-in support for the Shopify Template language ](/assets/media/manual-template-file.png "Built-in support for the Shopify Template language")

![Testspace pre-processes template files ](/assets/media/manual-template-test.png "Testspace pre-processes template files")

## Manual Execution integrated with GitHub Actions

Automated fixtures, leveraging GitHub Actions, can be integrated with Manual test execution. The automation ensures that the test is in a well-known state before a tester executes any instructions.  
  
Fixtures can reduce tedious and redundant setup/teardown execution requirements. Fixtures are serverless scripts that require no installation steps for testers.  
  
AWS Lambdas also work as fixtures.

[See details about Automated Fixtures](https://help.testspace.com/manual/implementation-spec#automated-fixtures)

![Automated Fixturing using GitHub Actions](/assets/media/manual-github-actions.png "Automated Fixturing using GitHub Actions")

## Resource Articles

![Implementing Manual Tests using GitHub Repositories](/assets/images/blog/single-post-headers/manual-test.jpg "Implementing Manual Tests using GitHub Repositories")

## Implementing Manual Tests using GitHub Repositories

Testspace automatically discovers manual tests that are captured using “simple” plain text [Markdown](https://docs.github.com/en/get-started/writing-on-github/getting-started-with-writing-and-formatting-on-github/basic-writing-and-formatting-syntax) files and are committed to a **repository**. This approach follows the same process as software development - version control, pull requests for reviews, etc. The files are rendered as test instructions, allowing human testers to execute and provide status.

[Read more](/blog/github-test-case-management)

![Visualizing GitHub Automated Test Results](/assets/images/blog/single-post-headers/github-actions.jpg "Visualizing GitHub Automated Test Results")

## Visualizing GitHub Automated Test Results

Testspace has built-in integrations with GitHub Actions. To publish content simply “push” file(s) with the Testspace CLI. Test results, code coverage, and other artifacts can be published using a single command.

[Read more](/blog/visualizing-test-results-with-github-actions)

![Managing Software Quality Under Continuous Integration](/assets/images/blog/single-post-headers/managing-quality-with-ci.png "Managing Software Quality Under Continuous Integration")

## Managing Software Quality Under Continuous Integration

Learn how Testspace simplifies managing software quality and release readiness, by leveraging important information buried in your CI/Build systems.

[Read more](/blog/managing-software-quality-under-continuous-integration)

## Test Management Software

[Live Dashboard](https://demo.testspace.com/)

Sign up with
or Email

results.title: 10 Github Repositories for Software Testers - DEV Community
results.content:
## 6. Test Cases and Templates for Manual Software Testing

The "Test Cases and Templates for Manual Software Testing" repository is a valuable resource for manual testers seeking to streamline their testing processes. It offers a comprehensive set of sample test cases and templates for test planning and bug bashes, simplifying and accelerating the test case creation process.

The project's primary aim is to address the time-consuming nature of writing test cases in software testing. By providing generic test cases for commonly used functionality, such as text boxes, dropdown boxes, checkboxes, and more, testers can readily download and employ them in their projects, saving substantial time that would otherwise be spent on crafting generic test cases. [...] ## 9. How They Test

The "How They Test" repository is a curated collection of publicly available resources that offer insights into how software companies worldwide approach testing and build a culture of quality. It serves as a comprehensive knowledge hub, consolidating best practices, tools, techniques, and testing culture from various companies, all accessible in one place. [...] The repository includes sample test cases and templates for a range of testing scenarios, including export and import functionalities, testing reports, mobile apps, login functionality, and a test planning checklist. It serves as a practical resource for manual testers, enhancing efficiency and promoting best practices in the software testing domain.

## 7. Quality in Mobile Apps

# 10 Github Repositories for Software Testers

Software testing is an integral part of the software development process, ensuring that the final product is reliable, bug-free, and meets user expectations. Manual testers play a crucial role in this journey, meticulously evaluating software to identify issues, report defects, and provide valuable feedback. To excel in their roles, testers often rely on a wide range of resources, from testing methodologies and roadmaps to helpful tools and educational materials.

In this article, we've curated a list of 10 Github repositories tailored to the needs of manual testers. These repositories are a treasure trove of information, offering roadmaps for career growth, book recommendations, course listings, and an assortment of tools to simplify the testing process. Whether you're a budding manual tester looking to start your journey or a seasoned professional seeking new resources, this collection will help you navigate the world of software testing and stay on the cutting edge of this dynamic field.

Let's dive into these repositories and explore the wealth of knowledge and tools they offer, helping you become a more effective and informed software tester.

## 1. [Awesome Quality Assurance Roadmap](https://github.com/fityanos/awesome-quality-assurance-roadmap)

The "Awesome Quality Assurance Roadmap" repository offers a structured path for QA engineers and software testers to enhance their skills. It provides a generic test plan sample, emphasizing the importance of effective test planning. Additionally, it shares expert advice, stressing the significance of understanding software testing fundamentals before diving into automation and broadening testing perspectives beyond server status codes. This resource equips testers with essential insights and resources to excel in their roles.

## 2. [Free Learning Resources For Software Testers](https://github.com/PaulWaltersDev/FreeLearningResourcesForSoftwareTesters)

The "Free Learning Resources For Software Testers" repository is an ongoing project that compiles free online learning materials for software testers, catering to both beginners and seasoned professionals. Recognizing the lack of dedicated software testing modules in computer science and IT departments, this project provides a structured curriculum and organized lists of relevant, non-promotional, and cost-free resources. While exceptions apply for paid resources like books and magazines, the repository serves as a valuable, ever-expanding resource for the testing community, encouraging contributions and offering a repository of free educational content.

## 3. [Awesome Learning](https://github.com/mfaisalkhatri/awesome-learning)

The "Awesome Learning" repository documents the remarkable journey of a software testing professional who transitioned from manual testing to a Test Automation Engineer over 14 years. Starting as a Commerce graduate in 2008 with no engineering background, the author provides insights into the challenges of understanding programming, testing, and software releases. This repository serves as a guide for those aspiring to make a similar career shift, offering a structured learning path based on the author's experience and knowledge, inspiring others to excel in test automation.

## 4. [SQA wiki](https://github.com/ligurio/sqa-wiki)

The "SQA wiki" repository is a collaborative platform that hosts a variety of resources focusing on software quality and testing including books in both English and Russian, learning courses in both languages, and tools that gamify the learning process. It encourages active participation, allowing contributors to edit existing articles and create new ones, fostering a community-driven knowledge base.

## 5. [Awesome Testing Courses & Tutorials](https://github.com/upgundecha/awesome-testing-courses)

“Awesome Testing Courses & Tutorials” provides A curated list of awesome online courses & tutorials on software testing, test automation and more.

## 6. [Test Cases and Templates for Manual Software Testing](https://github.com/mfaisalkhatri/Manual_Testing)

The "Test Cases and Templates for Manual Software Testing" repository is a valuable resource for manual testers seeking to streamline their testing processes. It offers a comprehensive set of sample test cases and templates for test planning and bug bashes, simplifying and accelerating the test case creation process.

The project's primary aim is to address the time-consuming nature of writing test cases in software testing. By providing generic test cases for commonly used functionality, such as text boxes, dropdown boxes, checkboxes, and more, testers can readily download and employ them in their projects, saving substantial time that would otherwise be spent on crafting generic test cases.

The repository includes sample test cases and templates for a range of testing scenarios, including export and import functionalities, testing reports, mobile apps, login functionality, and a test planning checklist. It serves as a practical resource for manual testers, enhancing efficiency and promoting best practices in the software testing domain.

## 7. [Quality in Mobile Apps](https://github.com/mgasiorowski/quality_mobile_apps)

The "Quality in Mobile Apps" repository is a one-stop destination for resources dedicated to mobile app testing, covering both iOS and Android platforms. It houses a diverse array of tools, guides, and references aimed at ensuring the high-quality performance of mobile applications. Whether you're a mobile app tester, developer, or quality assurance professional, this repository offers essential insights and tools to help you deliver top-notch mobile app experiences.

## 8. [Performance Testing](https://github.com/mgasiorowski/performance_testing)

The "Performance Testing" repository is a comprehensive resource for professionals looking to optimize software performance. It includes a wealth of tools and resources for both backend and frontend performance testing, as well as web performance optimization. This repository serves as a valuable guide to help individuals ensure their software applications run efficiently and deliver a seamless user experience.

## 9. [How They Test](https://github.com/abhivaikar/howtheytest)

The "How They Test" repository is a curated collection of publicly available resources that offer insights into how software companies worldwide approach testing and build a culture of quality. It serves as a comprehensive knowledge hub, consolidating best practices, tools, techniques, and testing culture from various companies, all accessible in one place.

This repository covers a wide array of topics, extending beyond traditional functional testing and test automation to include non-functional testing, testing in continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD), release management's impact on quality, quality processes, and even testing in production. Companies like Google, Netflix, and Amazon are among those sharing their valuable insights, making it an invaluable resource for those looking to learn and improve their software testing practices.

## 10. [Software testing resource pack](https://github.com/LukaszLapaj/software-testing-resource-pack)

The “Software testing resource pack” repository contains various files useful for manual testing and test automation to check REST API or webpage behaviour with various file extensions, sizes, resolution, names etc.
